emails.content:
David,

emails.subject: Financial Stewardship Small Group — Starting Thursday
emails.content:
Hey everyone,

Hope you all had a good Sunday. Quick note — we're kicking off a 6-week small group series this Thursday evening on Financial Stewardship. 7 PM at the church, same room as usual.

The idea is simple: honest conversations about how we manage what God gives us. Not a lecture, not a guilt trip. Just brothers walking through this together.

We'll have a different person share each week. Nothing fancy, just your story, your struggles, your wins. If you're interested in sharing during one of the sessions let me know.

First week is just a kickoff. Come ready to listen and be real.

Grace and peace,
Deon

emails.subject: Re: Financial Stewardship Small Group — Starting Thursday
emails.content:
Deon,

This sounds great. Count me in for Thursday.

I'd also be interested in sharing one of the weeks if there's still a slot open. Been doing some work on retirement planning lately and I think it could be a good conversation.

Isaiah

emails.subject: Re: Financial Stewardship Small Group — Starting Thursday
emails.content:
Isaiah,

That's awesome brother. I've got you down for Week 4, that would be December 4. Perfect timing actually, right before the holidays when everyone's thinking about money anyway.

No pressure on format. Just share what's on your heart. 10-15 minutes, then we open it up for discussion.

Looking forward to it.

Deon

emails.subject: Q4 Earnings - Need Your Eyes on Thornton
emails.content:
Isaiah,

Thornton Industrial just posted Q3. Revenue beat by 4%, but margins came in light. I've attached the earnings summary and the segment breakdown.

Need your take on whether we hold the position or trim before year-end. The Wellspring portfolio has 3.2% exposure and I'd like to have a recommendation ready for the morning standup.

Apologies for the late hour. Earnings season.

Garrett

emails.subject: Re: Q4 Earnings - Need Your Eyes on Thornton
emails.content:
Garrett,

Ran through the numbers. Here's where I land:

Revenue beat looks solid but the margin compression is real. Gross margin dropped 180bps YoY, mostly raw materials and freight. Management guided flat margins for Q4 which means no near-term relief.

For Wellspring:
- The 3.2% position is small enough that trimming creates more transaction cost than risk reduction
- Stock is trading at 14.8x forward, which prices in most of the margin pressure already
- I'd hold through Q4 and reassess after the January call

For the growth portfolio:
- We're slightly overweight industrials. If we want to trim somewhere, Thornton is the weakest name in the sector basket
- Could reallocate to the Agilent position we've been building

Recommendation is hold for Wellspring, consider a small trim in the growth book. Happy to walk through the model in the morning.

Isaiah

emails.subject: Re: Q4 Earnings - Need Your Eyes on Thornton
emails.content:
Good analysis. The Agilent swap is worth looking at.

Let's walk through it at standup. Get some rest.

Garrett

emails.subject: Picking your brain on retirement
emails.content:
Hey Isaiah,

Hope your new year is off to a good start. I've been thinking about what you shared in the small group back in December. The stuff about getting intentional with retirement planning really stuck with me.

I'm 38 and honestly haven't thought much about it beyond signing up for my company's 401k when I started. My wife and I were talking about it over the holidays and realized we don't really have a plan.

Would you be open to sharing some pointers? Even just where to start. No pressure at all, I know you're busy with work.

Thanks,
James

emails.subject: Re: Picking your brain on retirement
emails.content:
James,

Good to hear from you. Glad that resonated.

A few things that are solid starting points:

- If your company matches 401k contributions, make sure you're contributing at least up to the match. That's free money you're leaving on the table otherwise.
- At 38, a target-date fund (sometimes called a lifecycle fund) is a good hands-off option if you don't want to pick individual allocations. Most 401k plans have them. Look for one labeled 2050 or 2055.
- Start simple. Bumping your contribution by even 1-2% each year adds up more than you'd think over 20+ years.
- Stay away from anyone trying to sell you whole life insurance as a retirement vehicle. That's a red flag.

I should say upfront that I'm not a financial advisor and can't give specific recommendations. But happy to point you in the right direction.

Isaiah

emails.subject: Re: Picking your brain on retirement
emails.content:
Isaiah,

This is really helpful. I had no idea about the target date fund thing. I think I just picked some random funds when I set up my account 5 years ago and haven't looked at it since.

Quick question. My company matches up to 5% but I'm only putting in 3%. Should I jump straight to 5% or is it better to bump it up gradually?

Thanks again man.

James

emails.subject: Re: Picking your brain on retirement
emails.content:
James,

If you can swing it, go straight to 5. You're leaving money on the table at 3. But if the budget is tight, bumping 1% every quarter works too. Either way just get to the match.

One other thing. Log into your 401k portal and check the expense ratios on whatever funds you're in. If anything is over 1%, you might want to move to lower-cost index options. Small difference on paper but it compounds over decades.

Isaiah

emails.subject: Re: 2025 Tax Filing — Isaiah Harris
emails.content:
Angela,

March 15 at 4 works. I'll send docs over that morning.

Thanks,
Isaiah

emails.subject: Spring Gathering — Speaker Invitation
emails.content:
Hi Isaiah,

We're putting together the program for our Spring Gathering on April 5th, and Pastor Deon suggested your name for our "Real Talk on Stewardship" panel.

It would be a short 5-minute share — just your story, whatever feels authentic. Deon mentioned you've been really intentional about getting your finances in order lately, and we think that kind of testimony could encourage a lot of people in the congregation who are working through similar things.

Would you be open to it? If so, could you send me a few bullet points of what you'd cover so we can prep the program?

Blessings,
Sharon Williams
Events Coordinator
GracePoint Community Church

emails.subject: Lunch This Week?
emails.content:
Isaiah,

Let me take you to lunch one of these days. Your Q1 numbers are tracking ahead of benchmark and I want to celebrate that.

Also — and I'm not trying to pry — you've been heads-down more than usual lately. Turning down the Wellspring dinners, skipping the holiday party. I get it if you're just in the zone, but I want to make sure there's nothing going on that I should know about.

You've earned a meal. Pick the spot.

Garrett

emails.subject: Dinner Next Week?
emails.content:
Isaiah,

Been meaning to reach out since Garrett connected us last fall. I've been watching your mid-cap positioning and the Q4 performance was sharp.

I'm in Austin next Thursday for a couple meetings. Any chance you're free for dinner? Happy to pick the spot — my treat. We're looking at shifting some allocation and I'd love to get your read on a few names.

Also, if you're around this weekend, a few of us from the Austin finance crowd are doing a wine tasting at LeRoy's Saturday evening. Low-key, no shop talk. You'd fit right in.

Best,
David Chen
CIO, Wellspring Capital
